annotations.label: Sphere (set of equidistant points)

anchor.snippet: a sphere is the set of points that are all at the same distance

annotations.note: `Metric.sphere x ε := { y | dist y x = ε }` is the set of equidistant points in any (pseudo)metric space.

annotations.label: Center and radius

anchor.snippet: That given point is the center of the sphere, and the distance

annotations.note: The structure `EuclideanGeometry.Sphere` bundles fields `center : P` and `radius : ℝ`.

annotations.label: Radius (line segment)

anchor.snippet: any line from the center to a point on the sphere is also called a radius

annotations.note: Mathlib treats radius only as a scalar field of `EuclideanGeometry.Sphere`, not as a line segment from center to surface.

annotations.label: Diameter and antipodal points

anchor.snippet: If a radius is extended through the center to the opposite side of the sphere, it creates a diameter

annotations.note: The antipodal map on the unit sphere is formalized via negation (`contMDiff_neg_sphere`), but diameter as a chord/segment is not defined.

annotations.label: Unit sphere

anchor.snippet: A unit sphere is a sphere with unit radius

annotations.note: The unit sphere is denoted `Metric.sphere (0 : E) 1` throughout Mathlib (e.g. in `Mathlib.Geometry.Manifold.Instances.Sphere`).

annotations.label: Great circle and hemispheres

anchor.snippet: A great circle on the sphere has the same center and radius as the sphere

annotations.note: No definition of great circle or hemisphere on a sphere appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Axis, poles, equator, meridians, parallels

anchor.snippet: A particular line passing through its center defines an axis

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formal notions of axis, pole, equator, meridian, or parallel on a sphere.

annotations.label: Ball vs sphere

anchor.snippet: They draw a distinction between a sphere and a ball

annotations.note: Mathlib distinguishes `Metric.ball`, `Metric.closedBall` and `Metric.sphere` as separate notions.

annotations.label: Open and closed ball

anchor.snippet: An open ball excludes the sphere itself, while a closed ball includes the sphere

annotations.note: `Metric.ball` and `Metric.closedBall` are defined with the expected strict/non-strict distance inequalities.

annotations.label: Sphere in analytic geometry

anchor.snippet: a sphere with center

annotations.note: Solutions of `dist y x = ε` in `EuclideanSpace` give exactly the analytic-geometry sphere equation.

annotations.label: Sphere is a quadric surface

anchor.snippet: Since it can be expressed as a quadratic polynomial, a sphere is a quadric surface

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of quadric surfaces, so the sphere is not classified as one.

annotations.label: Imaginary sphere, point sphere, real sphere classification

anchor.snippet: has no real points as solutions

annotations.note: Emptiness for negative radius is captured by `sphere_eq_empty_of_neg`, but the imaginary/point/real trichotomy is not named.

annotations.label: Plane as a sphere of infinite radius

anchor.snippet: Thus, a plane may be thought of as a sphere of infinite radius

annotations.note: Mathlib does not treat planes as limiting spheres of infinite radius.

annotations.label: Parametric equation for the sphere

anchor.snippet: A parametric equation for the sphere with radius

annotations.note: The trigonometric latitude/longitude parametrization is not present; only stereographic charts are formalized.

annotations.label: Volume of a ball

anchor.snippet: the volume inside a sphere (that is, the volume of a ball, but classically referred to as the volume of a sphere) is

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace.volume_ball` gives the general n-dimensional formula using `Γ`, which specializes to `(4/3)πr³` in dimension 3.

annotations.label: Archimedes' sphere–cylinder volume relation

anchor.snippet: the volume inside a sphere is twice the volume between the sphere and the circumscribed cylinder

annotations.note: Archimedes' classical result that V(sphere) = 2·V(cylinder minus sphere) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sphere inscribed in a cube (52.4% volume)

anchor.snippet: the volume inside a sphere inscribed in a cube can be approximated as 52.4% of the volume of the cube

annotations.note: No statement comparing the ball volume to its bounding cube is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Surface area of a sphere

anchor.snippet: The surface area of a sphere of radius

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formula `4πr²` for the surface measure of the 2-sphere.

annotations.label: Surface area as derivative of volume

anchor.snippet: it equals the derivative of the formula for the volume with respect to r

annotations.note: The identity A(r) = dV/dr relating sphere surface area to ball volume is not formalized.

annotations.label: Isoperimetric property of the sphere

anchor.snippet: The sphere has the smallest surface area of all surfaces that enclose a given volume

annotations.note: The classical isoperimetric inequality is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Specific surface area

anchor.snippet: The surface area relative to the mass of a ball is called the specific surface area

annotations.note: Specific surface area is a physical quantity not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sphere as surface of revolution of a circle

anchor.snippet: A sphere can be constructed as the surface formed by rotating a circle one half revolution about any of its diameters

annotations.note: Mathlib has no surface-of-revolution construction generating the sphere.

annotations.label: Sphere determined by four non-coplanar points

anchor.snippet: A sphere is uniquely determined by four points that are not coplanar

annotations.note: The circumcenter of an affinely independent 3-simplex gives the unique circumsphere through its four vertices.

annotations.label: Sphere through a circle and a point

anchor.snippet: a sphere is uniquely determined by (that is, passes through) a circle and a point not in the plane of that circle

annotations.note: Mathlib does not state existence/uniqueness of a sphere through a given circle and external point.

annotations.label: Two spheres intersect in a circle (radical plane)

anchor.snippet: two spheres intersect in a circle and the plane containing that circle is called the radical plane

annotations.note: Mathlib has no result describing intersections of two spheres or a radical plane.

annotations.label: Angle between two spheres

anchor.snippet: The angle between two spheres at a real point of intersection is the dihedral angle

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define the dihedral angle between two intersecting spheres.

annotations.label: Orthogonality condition for two spheres

anchor.snippet: They intersect at right angles (are orthogonal) if and only if the square of the distance between their centers is equal to the sum of the squares of their radii

annotations.note: The Pythagorean orthogonality criterion d² = r₁²+r₂² is not formalized for spheres.

annotations.label: Pencil of spheres

anchor.snippet: The set of all spheres satisfying this equation is called a pencil of spheres

annotations.note: No notion of a pencil of spheres is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hilbert–Cohn-Vossen properties of the sphere

anchor.snippet: David Hilbert and Stephan Cohn-Vossen describe eleven properties of the sphere

annotations.note: The collected Hilbert–Cohn-Vossen list is not present as a theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: All points of a sphere are umbilics

anchor.snippet: All points of a sphere are umbilics

annotations.note: Mathlib has no theory of umbilical points or principal curvatures characterizing the sphere.

annotations.label: Sphere has constant mean curvature

anchor.snippet: The sphere has constant mean curvature

annotations.note: Mean curvature of surfaces is not formalized in Mathlib, so the constancy on the sphere is not stated.

annotations.label: Rotation group SO(3) acts on the sphere

anchor.snippet: this family is the rotation group SO(3)

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `SO(n)` (`Matrix.specialOrthogonalGroup`) and has the orthogonal action on Euclidean space; the smooth action on `S²` is not packaged as a named result.

annotations.label: Geodesic as great circle on the sphere

anchor.snippet: The analogue of the "line" is the geodesic, which is a great circle

annotations.note: Mathlib has no geodesic theory identifying great circles as geodesics on the sphere.

annotations.label: Shortest path on sphere is great-circle arc

anchor.snippet: Measuring by arc length shows that the shortest path between two points lying on the sphere is the shorter segment of the great circle that includes the points

annotations.note: Shortest-path/Riemannian-distance on the sphere is not formalized.

annotations.label: Sphere fails the parallel postulate

anchor.snippet: the sphere fails to satisfy some of classical geometry's postulates , including the parallel postulate

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formal spherical geometry, so the parallel postulate's failure on the sphere is not stated.

annotations.label: Spherical triangle angle sum exceeds 180°

anchor.snippet: the sum of the interior angles of a spherical triangle always exceeds 180 degrees

annotations.note: Mathlib has no spherical triangle theory or angle-excess theorem.

annotations.label: Similar spherical triangles are congruent

anchor.snippet: any two similar spherical triangles are congruent

annotations.note: No notion of similar/congruent spherical triangles is formalized.

annotations.label: Antipodal points on the sphere

anchor.snippet: Any pair of points on a sphere that lie on a straight line through the sphere's center

annotations.note: The antipodal map on the unit sphere is realized as negation `x ↦ -x` and shown to be smooth, but antipodal pairs are not packaged as a relation.

annotations.label: Gaussian curvature of the sphere

anchor.snippet: The sphere is a smooth surface with constant Gaussian curvature at each point equal to

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Gaussian curvature, so the constant-curvature property of the sphere is absent.

annotations.label: Theorema Egregium

anchor.snippet: As per Gauss's Theorema Egregium , this curvature is independent of the sphere's embedding

annotations.note: Gauss's Theorema Egregium (intrinsic invariance of Gaussian curvature) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sphere cannot be mapped to a plane preserving area and angle

anchor.snippet: a sphere cannot be mapped to a plane while maintaining both areas and angles

annotations.note: No non-existence theorem for area- and angle-preserving sphere-to-plane maps is present.

annotations.label: Area element of a sphere

anchor.snippet: A sphere of radius

annotations.note: The spherical area element `r² sin θ dθ dφ` is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sphere as integral surface of a differential form

anchor.snippet: A sphere of any radius centered at zero is an integral surface of the following differential form

annotations.note: Mathlib does not describe spheres as integral surfaces of a differential 1-form.

annotations.label: Filling area conjecture

anchor.snippet: the filling area conjecture states that the hemisphere is the optimal (least area) isometric filling of the Riemannian circle

annotations.note: The filling area conjecture is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sphere eversion

anchor.snippet: it is possible to turn an ordinary sphere inside out in a three-dimensional space

annotations.note: Smale's sphere eversion is not in Mathlib (it lives in a separate Lean project).

annotations.label: Real projective plane as antipodal quotient

anchor.snippet: The antipodal quotient of the sphere is the surface called the real projective plane

annotations.note: RP² as the antipodal quotient of S² is not constructed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intersection of a sphere and a plane

anchor.snippet: The intersection of a sphere and a plane is a circle, a point, or empty

annotations.note: No classification of plane-sphere intersections as circle/point/empty is formalized.

annotations.label: Great and small circles

anchor.snippet: Great circles are the intersection of the sphere with a plane passing through the center of a sphere

annotations.note: Mathlib defines neither great circles nor small circles on a sphere.

annotations.label: Sphere–cylinder intersection

anchor.snippet: the diagram to the right shows the intersection of a sphere and a cylinder, which consists of two circles

annotations.note: Sphere–cylinder intersection geometry is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Loxodrome (rhumb line)

anchor.snippet: a loxodrome or rhumb line is a path whose bearing

annotations.note: Loxodromes/rhumb lines are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Clelia curve

anchor.snippet: Another kind of spherical spiral is the Clelia curve

annotations.note: Clelia curves are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Viviani's curve as Clelia curve

anchor.snippet: Viviani's curve

annotations.note: Viviani's curve does not appear in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spherical conic

anchor.snippet: The analog of a conic section on the sphere is a spherical conic, a quartic curve

annotations.note: Spherical conics are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sphere–cylinder system intersection

anchor.section: Intersection of a sphere with a more general surface
anchor.snippet: The intersection of the sphere with equation

annotations.note: The specific sphere-with-surface intersection example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ellipsoid

anchor.snippet: An ellipsoid is a sphere that has been stretched or compressed in one or more directions

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `Ellipsoid` definition.

annotations.label: n-sphere

anchor.snippet: an n-sphere, often denoted

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `Metric.sphere (0 : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (n+1))) 1` as the n-sphere, as in `Mathlib.Geometry.Manifold.Instances.Sphere`.

annotations.label: n-sphere is a compact topological manifold

anchor.snippet: the n-sphere is an example of a compact topological manifold without boundary

annotations.note: The unit sphere in `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (n+1))` is given a `ChartedSpace` instance over `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)` and is compact via `isCompact_sphere`.

annotations.label: Exotic sphere

anchor.snippet: it need not be diffeomorphic to the Euclidean sphere (an exotic sphere

annotations.note: Exotic spheres (smooth manifolds homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic to the standard sphere) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: n-sphere is compact (Heine–Borel)

anchor.snippet: The sphere is the inverse image of a one-point set under the continuous function

annotations.note: `isCompact_sphere` proves any sphere is compact in a proper metric space, which includes finite-dimensional Euclidean space.

annotations.label: Sphere in a metric space

anchor.snippet: in a metric space

annotations.note: `Metric.sphere x ε` is defined directly for any pseudometric space.

annotations.label: Sphere in Z^n may be empty

anchor.snippet: in Z

annotations.note: Mathlib has emptiness lemmas like `sphere_eq_empty_of_subsingleton` and `sphere_eq_empty_of_neg`, but no specific example computing spheres in `ℤⁿ`.

annotations.label: Octahedron as taxicab sphere; cube as Chebyshev sphere

anchor.snippet: An octahedron is a sphere in taxicab geometry, and a cube is a sphere in geometry using the Chebyshev distance

annotations.note: Mathlib does not identify the L¹ unit sphere with an octahedron or the L∞ unit sphere with a cube.
